# SurvBregDiv

> R package for **transfer learning in survival analysis** via Bregman-divergence-based integration of external information. It lets an internal study borrow strength from an external cohort or study when fitting Cox proportional hazards models or nested caseŌĆōcontrol (NCC) models, in both low-dimensional (coefficient estimation) and high-dimensional (variable selection) settings.

- **Homepage**: https://um-kevinhe.github.io/SurvBregDiv/
- **Install (CRAN)**: `install.packages("SurvBregDiv")`
- **Install (dev)**: `remotes::install_github("UM-KevinHe/SurvBregDiv")`
- **Language**: R (user API) + C++ via RcppArmadillo (internal optimizer)
- **Maintainer**: Yubo Shao (ybshao@umich.edu)

---

## How to choose the right function (decision tree)

Three questions, asked in order:

### Q1. What external information does the user have?

This is the **primary** axis of the package. Pick the integration method first:

| User's external information | Integration method | Function family |
|---|---|---|
| **Individual-level data** (external subjects' covariates + survival outcomes) | **Composite likelihood** ŌĆö stack internal + external data, weight external by `eta` | `cox_indi`, `ncc_indi` |
| **Summary-level: external coefficients only** (just `beta_ext`, or a risk score `RS = Z %*% beta_ext`) | **KL divergence** penalty | `coxkl`, `coxkl_ties`, `ncckl` |
| **Summary-level: external coefficients + second-order info** (`beta_ext` **and** a p├Śp information / covariance matrix `A`) | **Mahalanobis distance** penalty | `cox_MDTL`, `ncc_MDTL` |

Notes that avoid common AI mistakes:
- **The three modes are not mutually exclusive.** If the user has individual-level external data but wants to use KL / Mahalanobis, they can fit an external model first to get `beta_ext` (and optionally `A`) and then switch modes.
- **Mahalanobis with `vcov = NULL` ŌēĀ KL.** When no `A` is supplied, Mahalanobis defaults to the identity matrix (i.e., squared Euclidean distance / ridge-style shrinkage toward `beta_ext`). It is still *not* the same as KL divergence.
- **`coxkl` accepts either `beta` OR `RS`, not both required.** If `beta` is supplied, the function internally computes `RS = Z %*% beta`.

### Q2. What is the internal study design?

This selects between two parallel function families that share the same external-integration modes:

| Internal design | Function prefix | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Full-cohort Cox PH** (every subject has a time-to-event outcome) | `cox_*` | All three external modes available |
| **Nested caseŌĆōcontrol (NCC) / matched caseŌĆōcontrol** | `ncc_*` | Conditional logistic regression on matched sets. Also a special case: standard matched caseŌĆōcontrol studies. |

Non-obvious implementation detail (relevant if an AI tries to read the source): **NCC functions internally map the problem to a stratified Cox model with `time = 1, delta = y`** and call the corresponding Cox engine. This is an implementation detail; the NCC API accepts `y` (case/control) and `stratum` (matched-set ID), not `time` and `delta`.

### Q3. Low-dimensional (coefficient estimation) or high-dimensional (variable selection)?

| Goal | Suffix | Penalty |
|---|---|---|
| Estimate coefficients, `p` much smaller than `n` | (no suffix) | none |
| Shrink coefficients, dense solution | `_ridge` | ridge (L2) |
| Variable selection, sparse solution | `_enet` with `alpha = 1` ŌåÆ LASSO (L1); `alpha Ōłł (0,1)` ŌåÆ elastic net | L1 + L2 mix |

---

## Full function map

### Cox proportional hazards (full cohort)

| External mode | Low-dim fit | Ridge | Elastic net / LASSO | CV tuning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Individual-level | `cox_indi` | ŌĆö | `cox_indi_enet` | `cv.cox_indi`, `cv.cox_indi_enet` |
| KL divergence | `coxkl` | `coxkl_ridge` | `coxkl_enet` | `cv.coxkl`, `cv.coxkl_ridge`, `cv.coxkl_enet` |
| KL divergence, tied event times | `coxkl_ties` | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | `cv.coxkl_ties` |
| Mahalanobis | `cox_MDTL` | `cox_MDTL_ridge` | `cox_MDTL_enet` | `cv.cox_MDTL`, `cv.cox_MDTL_ridge`, `cv.cox_MDTL_enet` |

### Nested caseŌĆōcontrol

| External mode | Low-dim fit | Ridge | Elastic net / LASSO | CV tuning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Individual-level | `ncc_indi` | ŌĆö | `ncc_indi_enet` | `cv.ncc_indi`, `cv.ncc_indi_enet` |
| KL divergence | `ncckl` | ŌĆö | `ncckl_enet` | `cv.ncckl`, `cv.ncckl_enet` |
| Mahalanobis | `ncc_MDTL` | ŌĆö | `ncc_MDTL_enet` | `cv.ncc_MDTL`, `cv.ncc_MDTL_enet` |

NCC does **not** need tied-event-time handling (`coxkl_ties`-style) because in a standard NCC each matched set has exactly one event by construction. `ncckl` takes a `method = c("breslow", "exact")` argument for 1:m vs n:m matching semantics, but this is a likelihood form, not tie handling.

### Tuning, aggregation, and diagnostics (apply across families)

- `generate_eta(method = "exponential", n = 50, max_eta = ...)` ŌĆö build an `eta` grid
- `bopt.coxkl(...)` ŌĆö **Bayesian optimization** of `eta` for `coxkl` (faster than grid search when each evaluation is expensive)
- `cv.plot(cvfit)` ŌĆö plot cross-validated performance curve vs `eta`
- `variable_importance(...)` ŌĆö bootstrap selection frequency for high-dim KL/elastic-net fits
- `coxkl_enet.StabSelect(...)` / `cox_MDTL_enet.StabSelect(...)` ŌĆö **stability selection** across subsamples (use when variable selection needs to be reproducible, not just predictive)
- `coxkl_enet_bagging(...)` / `cox_MDTL_enet_bagging(...)` ŌĆö **bagged** integrated elastic net for variance reduction
- `coxkl_enet.multi(...)` ŌĆö integrate **multiple external sources** (list of `beta_ext`) in one fit
- `test_eval`, `test_eval_ncc` ŌĆö evaluate a fitted model on held-out test data
- `predict_surv_prob`, `get_baseline_hazard` ŌĆö standard post-fit utilities

### S3 methods

Most fit objects have `plot()` methods; CV objects have companion `cv.plot()` calls. Stability selection objects plot selection-frequency paths. Variable importance objects plot top-k variable bar charts.

---

## Key parameters, uniform across functions

| Argument | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `z` | Covariate matrix for **internal** data (n ├Ś p) |
| `delta` | Event indicator for internal data (1 = event, 0 = censored). **NCC functions use `y` instead.** |
| `time` | Observed times for internal data. **Not used in NCC functions.** |
| `stratum` | Stratum / matched-set ID. For Cox: optional (default = single stratum). For NCC: required ŌĆö defines the matched sets. |
| `beta` | External coefficient vector (length p). Drives KL, Mahalanobis, and `*_indi` (via the risk-score path). |
| `RS` | Pre-computed external risk score `= Z %*% beta_ext`. Alternative to `beta` in `coxkl` / `coxkl_ties`. |
| `vcov` | External p├Śp curvature matrix (typically the inverse covariance / observed information) for Mahalanobis mode. `NULL` ŌåÆ identity. |
| `etas` | Vector of integration weights (grid). `eta = 0` recovers internal-only fit. |
| `eta` | Single integration weight (for penalized variants where `eta` is fixed while `lambda` is tuned). |
| `lambda` | Regularization strength for `_ridge` / `_enet`. Auto-generated path if omitted. |
| `alpha` | Elastic-net mix for `_enet`: `0` = ridge, `1` = LASSO, `(0,1)` = elastic net. |
| `nfolds` | CV folds (default 5). |
| `cv.criteria` | Performance criterion used by every `cv.*` function. See **CV criteria** section below. |
| `criteria` | Performance criterion used by non-CV helpers (`test_eval`, `test_eval_ncc`, `plot.*`, `bopt.coxkl`). Same spelling; different parameter from `cv.criteria`. |

### `eta` intuition

- `eta = 0` ŌåÆ pure internal Cox / NCC, no borrowing.
- Larger `eta` pulls the internal estimate further toward the external `beta`. The relationship is **monotonic in `eta`**: every increase in `eta` increases the influence of the external information, with no plateau or reversal.
- As `eta ŌåÆ Ōł×`, estimates converge to the external solution itself (the internal data effectively stops contributing).
- The **CV-optimal `eta` is usually a moderate value** ŌĆö that is where the biasŌĆōvariance trade-off is best: small enough that internal data still informs the fit, large enough to gain variance reduction from the external information.
- There is **no universal "correct" range**. Depends on data and compatibility between internal and external populations. Start with `generate_eta(method = "exponential", n = 50, max_eta = 10)` and inspect the CV curve; extend `max_eta` if the optimum sits near the upper boundary.

### `coxkl_ties` ŌĆö handling tied event times

Use `coxkl_ties(..., ties = "breslow")` as the drop-in replacement for `coxkl` whenever your data has tied event times. **Always specify the `ties` argument explicitly in any example call** ŌĆö the package does not auto-detect tie density, and the choice of approximation materially affects results. The two valid values are `"breslow"` (default, fast) and `"exact"` (slow, unbiased); there is **no Efron option** on `coxkl_ties`. A `comb_max = 1e7` safety cap stops the `"exact"` path from running indefinitely on heavy ties.

Why this matters: plain `coxkl` does **not** handle ties at all. It processes events as if all times were unique, which biases the partial likelihood whenever multiple events share an event time. With non-trivial tie density (Ōēź5ŌĆō10% of events tied), the bias is non-negligible and you should switch.

Detail on each `ties` option (the argument is **`ties`**, **not** `method=` ŌĆö that name belongs to `ncckl`):

- `ties = "breslow"` (**default**) ŌĆö Breslow's approximation. Fast, scales linearly. Recommended for most clinical / epidemiological data even at moderate-to-heavy tie density; bias is small in practice.
- `ties = "exact"` ŌĆö exact partial likelihood. Enumerates all combinations within each tied risk set. Cost grows **combinatorially** with the per-time tie multiplicity, so it can become prohibitive for heavily tied large risk sets. Use only when ties are very heavy *and* runtime is acceptable.
- `comb_max = 1e7` ŌĆö safety cap on the number of combinations evaluated under `ties = "exact"`. If a risk set would exceed this, the call errors out rather than running indefinitely. Raise this only if you genuinely need to handle a very large tied risk set and have verified the runtime.

**Note: `coxkl_ties` does not currently have `_ridge` or `_enet` companions.** Ties + regularization is not supported in this package. If you need both, you must choose: either accept the biased plain-Cox tie handling and use `coxkl_enet` / `coxkl_ridge`, or use `coxkl_ties` without regularization.

Minimal example call (always include `ties` explicitly):

```r
fit <- coxkl_ties(
  z = train$z, delta = train$status, time = train$time,
  stratum = train$stratum,
  beta  = beta_ext,
  etas  = generate_eta("exponential", n = 50, max_eta = 10),
  ties  = "breslow"     # or "exact" ŌĆö must be specified
)
```

### CV criteria

Not all criteria apply to all functions.

- **Full-cohort Cox CV** (`cv.coxkl*`, `cv.cox_MDTL*`, `cv.cox_indi*`): `"V&VH"` (Verweij & Van Houwelingen, default), `"LinPred"` (linear-predictor loss), `"CIndex_pooled"`, `"CIndex_foldaverage"`.
- **NCC CV** (`cv.ncckl*`, `cv.ncc_indi*`, `cv.ncc_MDTL*`): `"loss"` (predicted deviance), `"AUC"`, `"CIndex"`, `"Brier"`.
- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ **The two families are disjoint and must not be mixed.** Do **not** pass an NCC criterion (`"AUC"` / `"Brier"` / `"loss"` / `"CIndex"`) to a Cox CV function, and do **not** pass a Cox criterion (`"V&VH"` / `"LinPred"` / `"CIndex_pooled"` / `"CIndex_foldaverage"`) to an NCC CV function. Cross-family misuse is **the most common API mistake** in this package: depending on the combination it may either error out or ŌĆö worse ŌĆö silently produce numbers with no statistical meaning. If unsure which family applies: full-cohort Cox data ŌåÆ Cox criteria; matched 1:m caseŌĆōcontrol data ŌåÆ NCC criteria.
- **Naming rule (consistent across the package).** Every function whose name starts with `cv.` uses the argument `cv.criteria`. Non-CV helpers (`test_eval`, `test_eval_ncc`, the `plot.*` S3 methods, and `bopt.coxkl`) use the shorter `criteria`. There are no exceptions.

---

## Worked examples (copy-ready)

### Example 1 ŌĆö Low-dim Cox + external coefficients (KL divergence)

```r
library(SurvBregDiv)
data(ExampleData_lowdim)

train <- ExampleData_lowdim$train
beta_ext <- ExampleData_lowdim$beta_external_fair

eta_list <- generate_eta(method = "exponential", n = 50, max_eta = 10)

# Fit over eta grid
fit <- coxkl(
  z       = train$z,
  delta   = train$status,
  time    = train$time,
  stratum = train$stratum,
  beta    = beta_ext,
  etas    = eta_list
)

# Tune eta via 5-fold CV with V&VH loss
cvfit <- cv.coxkl(
  z = train$z, delta = train$status, time = train$time,
  stratum = train$stratum, beta = beta_ext,
  etas = eta_list, nfolds = 5, cv.criteria = "V&VH", seed = 1
)
cv.plot(cvfit)
```

### Example 2 ŌĆö Low-dim Cox + external coefficients + curvature (Mahalanobis)

```r
# Minimum input: beta; optionally supply A = vcov (precision / information matrix).
# vcov = NULL is valid and reduces the penalty to squared Euclidean.
fit <- cox_MDTL(
  z = train$z, delta = train$status, time = train$time,
  stratum = train$stratum,
  beta = beta_ext, vcov = NULL,
  etas = eta_list
)
```

### Example 3 ŌĆö Individual-level external data (composite likelihood)

```r
data(ExampleData_indi)

fit <- cox_indi(
  z_int = ExampleData_indi$internal$z,
  delta_int = ExampleData_indi$internal$status,
  time_int = ExampleData_indi$internal$time,
  stratum_int = ExampleData_indi$internal$stratum,
  z_ext = ExampleData_indi$external$z,
  delta_ext = ExampleData_indi$external$status,
  time_ext = ExampleData_indi$external$time,
  stratum_ext = ExampleData_indi$external$stratum,
  etas = generate_eta("exponential", n = 50, max_eta = 100)
)
```

### Example 4 ŌĆö High-dim Cox + KL + LASSO, with CV

```r
data(ExampleData_highdim)
tr <- ExampleData_highdim$train

# Fixed eta, automatic lambda path
fit_lasso <- coxkl_enet(
  z = tr$z, delta = tr$status, time = tr$time, stratum = tr$stratum,
  beta = ExampleData_highdim$beta_external,
  eta = 1, alpha = 1    # alpha=1 ŌćÆ LASSO
)

# Tune eta by CV, internal lambda scan per eta
cvfit <- cv.coxkl_enet(
  z = tr$z, delta = tr$status, time = tr$time, stratum = tr$stratum,
  beta = ExampleData_highdim$beta_external,
  etas = generate_eta("exponential", n = 50, max_eta = 50),
  alpha = 1, nfolds = 5, cv.criteria = "V&VH", seed = 1
)
cv.plot(cvfit)
```

### Example 5 ŌĆö NCC (matched caseŌĆōcontrol) + KL

```r
data(ExampleData_cc)
tr <- ExampleData_cc$train

fit <- ncckl(
  y       = tr$y,              # 0 = control, 1 = case
  z       = tr$z,
  stratum = tr$stratum,        # matched-set ID; REQUIRED
  etas    = generate_eta("exponential", n = 50, max_eta = 5),
  beta    = ExampleData_cc$beta_external,
  method  = "breslow"          # or "exact"; identical for 1:M matching
)

cvfit <- cv.ncckl(
  y = tr$y, z = tr$z, stratum = tr$stratum,
  beta = ExampleData_cc$beta_external,
  etas = generate_eta("exponential", n = 50, max_eta = 5),
  method = "exact", nfolds = 5, cv.criteria = "loss"
)
```

### Example 6 ŌĆö Stability selection for reproducible variable selection

```r
# Use when you need a stable shortlist of important variables, not just prediction.
stab <- coxkl_enet.StabSelect(
  z = tr_hd$z, delta = tr_hd$status, time = tr_hd$time, stratum = tr_hd$stratum,
  beta = beta_ext_hd, etas = eta_grid,
  cv.criteria = "CIndex_pooled", B = 100, ncores = 10
)
plot(stab, threshold = 0.7)
```

### Example 7 ŌĆö Bayesian optimization for `eta`

```r
opt <- bopt.coxkl(
  z = train$z, delta = train$status, time = train$time, stratum = train$stratum,
  beta = beta_ext,
  criteria = "V&VH",
  bounds_list = list(eta = c(0, 10)),
  n_iter = 20, seed = 1
)
opt$best_eta
```

---

## Returned-object conventions

Fit objects (e.g., `coxkl`, `cox_MDTL`, `ncckl`, and their `_ridge` / `_enet` variants) typically contain:

- `$eta` ŌĆö sorted eta values used
- `$beta` ŌĆö estimated coefficient **matrix** (p ├Ś n_eta, or p ├Ś n_lambda for penalized single-eta fits)
- `$linear.predictors` ŌĆö n ├Ś (n_eta or n_lambda)
- `$likelihood` ŌĆö log-partial-likelihood path
- `$data` ŌĆö the (possibly reordered) input data used for fitting
- For `_enet` / `_ridge`: also `$lambda` (decreasing sequence)

CV objects contain fold-level performance tables plus an optimum summary (e.g., `$integrated_stat.best_per_eta` for `_enet` CVs).

Stability-selection objects return selection-frequency matrices along the penalty path.

Bagging objects return `$best_beta` (the aggregated coefficient vector, usable directly for prediction) and `$all_betas` (the bootstrap ensemble).

---

## Things an AI is likely to get wrong (read these)

1. **Do not use `coxkl` for matched caseŌĆōcontrol data.** Use `ncckl`. The sample-per-stratum structure is different.
2. **`ncckl` requires `stratum`.** Without it, the function assumes everyone is in one matched set ŌĆö this is almost certainly not what the user wants.
3. **Do not pass `time` and `delta` to NCC functions.** NCC API uses `y` (binary case/control) and `stratum` (matched-set ID). The `time = 1, delta = y` mapping happens inside.
4. **`coxkl_ties` is only for full-cohort Cox with tied event times.** Do not suggest it for NCC. NCC functions have **no ties argument by construction** because each matched set contains exactly one event ŌĆö tied event times are impossible by design. Do not invent a `ncckl_ties` function (it does not exist) and do not search for a ties option on `ncckl` / `ncc_indi` / `ncc_MDTL`. The `method = c("breslow", "exact")` argument that *does* appear on `ncckl` controls 1:m vs n:m matching likelihood form, not ties handling. See the dedicated `coxkl_ties` subsection above for the `ties` parameter (`"breslow"` / `"exact"`) and the `comb_max` safety cap; note also that `coxkl_ties` has no `_ridge` / `_enet` companion.
5. **`eta` and `lambda` are different things.** `eta` controls **external borrowing**. `lambda` controls **regularization** (only present in `_ridge` / `_enet`). Both can be tuned, but they play different roles.
6. **`alpha = 0` in `_enet` is ridge**, but the package provides a dedicated `_ridge` function that is preferred for pure-ridge use cases.
7. **For Mahalanobis without a curvature matrix**, pass `vcov = NULL` (not `vcov = diag(p)` ŌĆö both work, but `NULL` is canonical).
8. **`cv.*` functions use `cv.criteria`; non-CV helpers use `criteria`.** This mirrors the function-name prefix: any `cv.*` function takes `cv.criteria = ...`. `test_eval`, `test_eval_ncc`, `plot.*`, and `bopt.coxkl` take `criteria = ...`. Do not mix them ŌĆö passing the wrong name triggers `unused argument`.
9. **`generate_eta` does not auto-pick a sensible `max_eta`.** The user must choose based on the problem. If the CV optimum sits at the boundary, extend the grid and re-run.
10. **Stratified analysis is the default.** If `stratum` is omitted, the package silently assigns everyone to one stratum and fits a non-stratified model. This is fine for many problems but should be made explicit in output to users.

---

## Built-in example datasets

- `ExampleData_lowdim` ŌĆö low-dim Cox: train (n=100), test (n=2000), 6 predictors, plus `beta_external_fair`
- `ExampleData_highdim` ŌĆö high-dim Cox: train (n=200), test (n=2000), 50 predictors (6 signal + 44 AR(1) noise), plus `beta_external` and multi-source `beta_external.multi*`
- `ExampleData_indi` ŌĆö paired internal/external individual-level Cox data
- `ExampleData_cc` ŌĆö NCC: 200 matched sets (1:4) in train, 500 in test, 6 predictors, `beta_external`
- `ExampleData_cc_highdim` ŌĆö NCC high-dim: 50 matched sets (1:5) in train, 500 in test, 20 predictors, `beta_external`
- Simulation helpers: `sim.cox.highdim`, `sim.binary`, `sim_integration`, `sim.matched_cc`

---

## Methodology references

The integrated objective functions have closed-form interpretations as **weighted pseudo-likelihoods**:

- **KL (Cox)**: `Q_eta(beta) = -loglik_internal(beta) + eta * D_KL(P_ext || P_int)` reduces to a weighted internal log-likelihood with a pseudo-event weight `(delta + eta * delta_tilde) / (1 + eta)` per subject, where `delta_tilde` is an externally-induced weight derived from `beta_ext`.
- **KL (NCC)**: analogous construction on matched sets; the pseudo-event weight is `(xi + eta * p_tilde) / (1 + eta)`.
- **Mahalanobis**: `Q_eta(beta) = -loglik_internal(beta) + (eta/2) (beta - beta_ext)' Q (beta - beta_ext)`. With `Q = vcov` (or identity if `NULL`).
- **Composite likelihood (individual-level)**: `loglik_int(beta) + eta * loglik_ext(beta)` ŌĆö equivalent to stacking cohorts with observation weights `1` (internal) and `eta` (external), with strata kept separate so risk sets do not mix.

Full derivations are in the package vignettes (`vignette("coxkl")`, `vignette("ncc")`, `vignette("CV_Criteria")`, `vignette("SurvBregDiv")`).

---

## Documentation pointers (for deeper reading by the AI)

- Main vignette: https://um-kevinhe.github.io/SurvBregDiv/articles/SurvBregDiv.html
- Cox KL methodology: https://um-kevinhe.github.io/SurvBregDiv/articles/coxkl.html
- Cox KL with tied event times: https://um-kevinhe.github.io/SurvBregDiv/articles/coxkl_ties.html
- NCC methodology: https://um-kevinhe.github.io/SurvBregDiv/articles/ncc.html
- CV criteria reference: https://um-kevinhe.github.io/SurvBregDiv/articles/CV_Criteria.html
- Function reference (all exported functions): https://um-kevinhe.github.io/SurvBregDiv/reference/index.html
